cheatsheet.title: Simplifying the Eclipse Platform Status API

intro.description: This cheat sheet guides you through using the Platform Status Cleanup to simplify verbose new Status(...) constructions with the concise factory methods available in recent Eclipse versions when running on Java 9 or higher.

item.title: Understand the Old vs New Status API
item.description:
Before these factory methods were available, creating a status object required a verbose constructor call:
new Status(IStatus.ERROR, Activator.PLUGIN_ID, IStatus.OK, message, exception)
With these factory methods, concise replacements are available:
• Status.error(message)
• Status.error(message, exception)
• Status.warning(message)
• Status.info(message)
The cleanup replaces the old constructor patterns with these factory methods, reducing boilerplate and improving readability.

item.title: Enable the Cleanup
item.description:
Open the Clean Up dialog to enable the Platform Status cleanup:
1. In the Package Explorer, select an Eclipse plugin project
2. Choose Source → Clean Up…
3. Select Use custom profile and click Configure…
4. On the Platform Status (Sandbox) tab, enable Simplify Status creation
5. Click OK
Click Perform to open the Source menu.

item.title: Run the Cleanup
item.description:
Apply the cleanup to your plugin source files:
1. Open or select Java files that create Status objects using the old constructor
2. Choose Source → Clean Up…
3. Confirm the profile has Platform Status (Sandbox) enabled
4. Click Finish
The cleanup processes all matching constructor calls and replaces them with factory method invocations.

item.title: Review the Simplified Status Calls
item.description:
After the cleanup runs, inspect the updated code:
• new Status(IStatus.ERROR, pluginId, message) → Status.error(message)
• new Status(IStatus.ERROR, pluginId, message, e) → Status.error(message, e)
• new Status(IStatus.WARNING, pluginId, message) → Status.warning(message)
• new Status(IStatus.INFO, pluginId, message) → Status.info(message)
Note: The plug-in ID argument is omitted in the factory methods because they automatically determine the caller plug-in ID. Verify the build still passes after the cleanup.
